blurb: The best-tasting culinary marigold: a compact bushy mound of ferny, lemon-scented foliage smothered all summer in small, single citrus-daisy flowers — dainty where the big French and African types are bold, and far tastier.
water: Low–moderate; fairly drought-tolerant once established, water in dry spells
spread: Mounding, tidy clump; self-sows lightly from dropped seed
seasons: Masses of small single yellow-to-orange daisies from early summer to frost over lacy, aromatic lemon-scented foliage; killed outright by the first hard frost
wildlife: The small, open single flowers are easy, accessible forage for small native bees, hoverflies, and butterflies through a long season into fall
deer: Seldom browsed by deer — the pungent aromatic foliage is generally passed over
toxic: Safe and edible in moderation (flowers and young leaves); the foliage's phototoxic thiophenes can cause minor, usually brief skin irritation in sensitive people
habitat: Open, sunny disturbed ground in warm uplands

care.hardiness: Grown as a frost-tender warm-season annual — it completes its life in one season and is killed by the first hard frost, so winter hardiness is not a factor on the Front Range; it can be grown in essentially any zone as a summer annual.[1][2]
care.planting: A heat-lover — do not rush it out. Set out transplants or direct-sow only after the mid-May last frost in Boulder, once soil and nights have warmed; cold soil stalls it and frost kills it. Start seed indoors about 4–6 weeks before that date to get a jump. Plant into a sunny, well-drained bed. It excels in containers (a natural for a sunny patio or a kitchen-garden pot): use a free-draining mix in a pot with drainage holes, site in full sun, and expect to water often in summer heat and wind, as pots dry far faster than beds. Potted plants finish at the first fall frost with no overwintering.[1][4]
care.sun: Full sun — 6+ hours of direct sun gives the sturdiest, most free-flowering plants. It will not tolerate shade: in too little light it stretches, flops, and blooms poorly.[1][2]
care.soil: Adaptable to clay, loam, or sand; wants average, well-drained soil and tolerates a wide pH from acidic to alkaline (about 6.0–8.0), so Boulder's alkaline clay is fine where drainage is decent. Keep it lean — rich, over-fertilized ground produces leafy plants with few flowers.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate. Water to establish and through peak summer heat, letting the surface dry slightly between waterings; once rooted in it tolerates dry spells and is fairly drought-tolerant. Avoid overwatering — soggy soil rots the compact types and invites root disease; water at the base to keep foliage dry.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants about 8–12 in apart — these are compact, bushy little marigolds, so closer spacing than the big French/African types gives a solid, mounded edging or mat.[2]
care.propagation: Seed only — this annual is not divided or grown from cuttings. Start seed indoors 4–6 weeks before the mid-May frost, or direct-sow into warm soil after frost; it germinates fast, in about a week at 70–75°F. You can save your own seed from dried heads, but seed from named hybrid strains may not come true.[1][2]
care.sow: Direct-sow into warm soil after the mid-May last frost, or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier and transplant out once nights have warmed; expect germination in roughly a week at 70–75°F.[2]
care.depth: Sow seed shallowly, about ¼ in deep, and keep the surface warm and evenly moist for quick, even germination.[2]
care.bloom: Early summer to the first fall frost on the Front Range — roughly May–June into October — with the heaviest, most continuous bloom on plants that are regularly deadheaded.[1][4]
care.feeding: Little to none. Lean soil suits it best; over-feeding, especially high nitrogen, pushes lush foliage at the expense of flowers. A single light feed at planting is plenty on poor ground.[2]
care.maintenance: Deadhead spent flowers to keep bloom coming, and shear the plant back lightly if it goes leggy or tired in midsummer heat to force a fresh flush of foliage and flowers. It self-sows lightly where seed drops onto bare soil, but volunteers are easy to recognize and pull — it is not weedy or aggressive.[1][2]
care.troubles: Generally trouble-free. Spider mites are the most likely pest, flaring in hot, dry weather (usually minor); watch too for occasional botrytis blight or stem rot in wet spells and slugs on young seedlings. Good airflow, full sun, and base watering keep it clean.[2]

edible.summary: The premier culinary marigold — the small single flowers carry a genuine citrus-to-tarragon flavor (milder and better than African or French types) and the lacy young leaves are used too; a favorite edible flower.[1][3]
edible.parts: The colorful ray petals are the prize: pulled from the bitter green base and scattered fresh over salads, rice, fruit, and drinks, worked into baked goods, or dried for tea; the flavor runs citrusy-lemon to spicy tarragon and is notably milder than African marigold (Tagetes erecta). The finely divided, lemon-scented young leaves are used sparingly as a herb/garnish as well. Remove the stems, anthers, and pistils, which can be bitter.[1][3][4]
edible.preparation: Use only unsprayed, pesticide-free flowers you have grown yourself — never blooms from florists, garden centers, or nurseries, which are routinely treated. Pull the petals from the calyx, rinse, and use them fresh or dried, sparingly, as a garnish or flavoring rather than as a bulk vegetable. Introduce any new edible flower into your diet slowly and in small amounts.[3]
edible.caution: Eat in moderation — the petals can be harmful in large quantities, so treat them as a garnish, not a staple. Tagetes foliage, flowers, and roots contain phototoxic thiophene compounds that can cause minor, usually brief skin irritation in sensitive people (wear gloves if you react, especially on broken skin followed by sun exposure). Anyone with a known allergy to Asteraceae (daisy-family) plants should avoid eating it. Always be certain of your identification and that the plants were never sprayed.[1][3]
